explicit_text: I was born in California in 1944, specifically in Turlock, which is in the Central Valley. Growing up in California meant being around a lot of farm life and hard work, especially in my early years. Later, my family moved to the San Francisco Bay Area, where I spent my high school years and started my career.

explicit_text: I was born in Turlock, California, in 1944. It’s a small town in the Central Valley where I grew up surrounded by farm life. As a kid, I did a lot of hunting, fishing, and helping my dad with tractor work and hauling hay, which was tough but rewarding.

explicit_text: The Central Valley is where I spent my early childhood, growing up in Turlock. It was a farming region, and my family was involved in contract work and hay baling. Life there was all about hard work on the land, but it was a great place to grow up with plenty of outdoor activities.

explicit_text: My dad’s parents came from Australia, so that’s part of my family background. It’s interesting because most people assume European roots, but my paternal side is Australian. This heritage was always part of my family story growing up.

explicit_text: On my mom’s side, we’re basically Irish, though I’m not exactly sure when or how they came over. My family has been in the United States for a long time, probably since the early days of settlement. So, Irish roots are definitely part of my heritage.

explicit_text: When I was about 14 or 15, my family moved to the San Francisco Bay Area, specifically south of San Francisco in Redwood City. This move was because my dad switched from seasonal farm work to construction, which was more stable. I went to high school there before quitting at 16 to start working.

explicit_text: Redwood City is where I spent my high school years after moving from Turlock. I went to school there for a couple of years before quitting at 16 to become a carpenter apprentice. Later, I worked at a Montgomery Ward warehouse located in Redwood City before joining the waterfront.

explicit_text: I joined the United States Navy at 17 in 1961, following my brother’s footsteps and wanting to see the world. I served as a diesel mechanic on a fleet tug, doing engine room and refrigeration maintenance. I stayed on the same ship for my entire service until I got out in 1966, after an extension due to the Kennedy assassination.

I served as a diesel mechanic on an ocean-going tug, doing engine room and refrigeration maintenance. I stayed on the same ship for my entire service until I got out in 1966, after an extension due to the assassination of the president.

explicit_text: Although I did four tours to Vietnam, technically the war hadn’t officially started when I was there, according to records. My ship took supplies up the rivers and we got shot at a few times by snipers, but we never saw direct combat. We spent time in places like Saigon and had R&R stops in Hong Kong and Hawaii during the conflict.

explicit_text: The assassination of John F. Kennedy in 1963 had a direct impact on my Navy service because my discharge was extended by four months due to the event. I was supposed to get out in September 1963, but the extension kept me on the ship during a Vietnam deployment. It was a strange time, and that extension meant I got to see Vietnam firsthand before leaving the Navy.

explicit_text: After leaving the Navy in 1966, I worked in the warehouse at Montgomery Ward in Redwood City. It was a non-union job and pretty low-key, just me and an older lady doing pricing and warehouse work. It was a step before I moved on to the waterfront and the ILWU.

explicit_text: I joined the ILWU in 1967 when I started working on the waterfront. It was my first union job, and I quickly learned about the unique culture there. The union was a different world with its own politics and social justice causes, and I became comfortable working alongside the longshoremen despite some old-timers being tough on newcomers.

explicit_text: I didn’t meet Jim Brown right away after joining the waterfront. We actually connected in the early 1970s through a bar I owned that was across the street from the union hall. Jim Brown eventually bought the bar from another longshoreman, Jim Fowler, and that’s how our friendship started.
